documents.text: Arjuna appears in this passage of the Mahabharata under several epithets used by the narrator: "the diadem-decked (Arjuna)", "the ape-bannered Arjuna" (from the emblem of Hanuman on his banner), "Phalguni", "Dhananjaya the son of Pandu", "Vibhatsu", and "the wielder of Gandiva" (his bow), "that chastiser of foes...who was capable of drawing the bow with even his left hand."
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: Krishna, "cheerfully addressing Dhananjaya," told Arjuna: "There, Bhishma, the son of Santanu, stayeth between the two armies. Slaying him by putting forth thy might, thou mayst win victory. There, at that spot, whence he breaketh our ranks, check him, putting forth thy strength." "Thus urged, the ape-bannered Arjuna at that moment made Bhishma with his car, steeds, and standard, invisible by means of his arrows."
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: Because Bhishma had vowed not to raise weapons against a warrior who had formerly been born a woman, Arjuna fought from behind Sikhandin: "Sikhandin, however, that foremost of car-warriors, protected in the battle by the diadem-decked (Arjuna)..." and again, "the diadem-decked (Arjuna), excited with rage and placing Sikhandin to the fore, approached Bhishma (nearer)." The text presents this as a considered battlefield tactic against a vow, not an act the narrator condemns or praises outright — later Mahabharata tradition treats it as morally difficult.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: "Then Vibhatsu, drawing his bow Gandiva with a laugh, pierced the son of Ganga with five and twenty arrows. And once more, Dhananjaya, with great speed and excited with wrath struck him in every vital part with hundreds of arrows...the son of Pandu cut off in that battle even all the bows of Bhishma" — cutting three successive bows from Bhishma's hands with precisely aimed arrows rather than killing him outright.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: Bhishma himself testifies to Arjuna's unmatched skill even while dying from his wounds: "He is incapable of being vanquished in battle by the wielder of the thunder-bolt himself. As regards myself also, O hero, the very gods, Danavas and Rakshasas united together, are incapable of vanquishing me. What I shall say then of mighty car-warriors among men?" — acknowledging Arjuna as the one warrior capable of doing what was done.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: This same Book of the Mahabharata (the Bhishma Parva) also contains the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna's counsel to a hesitant Arjuna just before this battle begins, on duty, grief, and right action. The Gita is treated as a separate, already-sourced corpus in this app (the Annie Besant public domain translation) and is not reproduced here; this entry covers only Arjuna's role in the battle narrative that follows it.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: After Bhishma fell, "the heroic Pandavas possessed of massive arms that looked like spiked maces, having obtained the victory and won a highly blessed state in the other world, all blew their great conches." The text records mixed reactions across both armies — grief, shouting, and reflection — rather than simple celebration; Bhishma himself continued to be honored by both sides even after his fall.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: Arjuna (Dhananjaya, Phalguni, Vibhatsu), third of the Pandava brothers, is a central figure of the Mahabharata's Bhishma Parva (Book 6). This entry draws only on Sections CXIX-CXX of the Ganguli translation (1883-1896), public domain, digitized jointly by sacred-texts.com and Project Gutenberg. It does not cover the Bhagavad Gita itself, which is a separate already-sourced corpus, nor other Parvas of the epic.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: "...thy sire Bhishma, influenced by wrath, began to fight with the Srinjayas...the old Kuru grandsire Bhishma, day after day, slew ten thousand warriors of the Ratha. On the tenth day, however...Bhishma, single-handed, slew ten thousand elephants...none of the kings, O monarch, could even look at him, for he then resembled the hot mid-day Sun in the summer sky."
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: Krishna, seeing Bhishma break the Pandava ranks, said to Arjuna: "There, Bhishma, the son of Santanu, stayeth between the two armies. Slaying him by putting forth thy might, thou mayst win victory." Bhishma fought on the Kaurava side out of a prior vow of loyalty to the throne of Hastinapura, not from personal enmity toward the Pandavas, whom the text elsewhere calls his own kin.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: "...he continued...'With only a single bow I could slay all the Pandavas, if the mighty Vishnu himself had not been their protector. For two reasons, however, I will not fight with the Pandavas, viz., their unslayableness, and the femininity of Sikhandin... I should now wish my own death, this being the proper hour.'" Bhishma restrained his own full strength against the Pandavas by choice, even while continuing to fight for the Kaurava army.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: "...he fell down from his car with his head to the east, a little before sunset...That foremost of all bowmen, that mighty-armed hero, fell down, like an uprooted standard of Indra, making the earth tremble the while. Pierced all over with arrows, his body touched not the ground. At that moment...a divine nature took possession of that great bowman lying on a bed of arrows."
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: "The boon that was granted to me by my illustrious sire, to the effect that my death would depend on my own wish O, let that boon become true. I will hold my life, since I have control in the matter of laying it down... I will never pass out (of the world) as long as the Sun is in the southern solstice... I will proceed to my own ancient abode when the Sun reacheth the northern solstice." Bhishma lay on his bed of arrows for many weeks, by his own account, until the turning of the season, so as to die at an auspicious time.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: Of the arrows piercing him, Bhishma told Dussasana: "These arrows coursing towards me in one continuous line, whose touch resembleth that of heaven's bolt, have been shot by Arjuna. These are not Sikhandin's... Save the heroic wielder of Gandiva, viz., the ape-bannered Jishnu, even all other kings united together cannot cause me pain." Even mortally wounded, Bhishma names and honors the skill of the warrior who defeated him rather than complaining of his fate.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: "When the grandsire of the Bharatas who was endued with great might was overthrown, thy son...knew not what to do. And all the Kurus were entirely deprived of their senses... And the Rishis and the Pitris all applauded Bhishma of high vows. And the deceased ancestors of the Bharatas also praised Bhishma." Bhishma, endued with great ascetic merit, "having recourse to that Yoga which is taught in the great Upanishads and engaged in mental prayers, remained quiet, expectant of his hour" even after his fall.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: Bhishma (Devavrata, son of Ganga and Santanu) is a central figure of the Mahabharata's Bhishma Parva (Book 6), which also contains the Bhagavad Gita (a separate, already-sourced corpus in this app). This entry draws only on Sections CXIX-CXX of the Ganguli translation (1883-1896), public domain, digitized jointly by sacred-texts.com and Project Gutenberg. It does not cover Bhishma's earlier vow of lifelong celibacy (Adi Parva, Book 1), which is not yet sourced for this app.
documents.source_name: The Mahabharata, Book 6 (Bhishma Parva), translated by Kisari Mohan Ganguli (1883-1896)

documents.text: Guru Gobind Singh Ji was the tenth Sikh Guru, a spiritual master, warrior, poet and philosopher.
documents.source_name: Sikh History

documents.text: He founded the Khalsa in 1699, initiating the Panj Pyare and giving them the title of Singh.
documents.source_name: Sikh History

documents.text: He fought many battles against the Mughals to protect the oppressed and established a strong martial tradition.
documents.source_name: Sikh History

documents.text: "...the Venerable Ascetic Mahavira, the last of the Tirthakaras...took the form of an embryo in the womb of Devananda...in the brahmanical part of the town Kundagrama." By the god Sakra's order, the embryo was then transferred to the womb of "the Kshatriyani Trisala...wife of the Kshatriya Siddhartha," since it is held that Arhats are never born from a brahmanical womb, only a noble Kshatriya one.
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

documents.text: "The Venerable Ascetic Mahavira belonged to the Kasyapa gotra. His three names have thus been recorded: by his parents he was called Vardhamana; because he is devoid of love and hate, he is called Sramana (i.e. Ascetic); because he stands fast in midst of dangers and fears, patiently bears hardships and calamities, adheres to the chosen rules of penance, is wise, indifferent to pleasure and pain, rich in control, and gifted with fortitude, the name Venerable Ascetic Mahavira has been given him by the gods."
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

documents.text: At age thirty, after his parents' death, Mahavira renounced the world: "He left his silver, he left his gold, he left his riches, corn, majesty, and kingdom...he distributed presents through proper persons, he distributed presents among indigent persons." He then "descended from his palankin, took off his ornaments, garlands, and finery with his own hands, and with his own hands plucked out his hair in five handfuls," fasting two and a half days before entering "the state of houselessness."
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

documents.text: "The Venerable Ascetic Mahavira for a year and a month wore clothes; after that time he walked about naked, and accepted the alms in the hollow of his hand. For more than twelve years the Venerable Ascetic Mahavira neglected his body and abandoned the care of it; he with equanimity bore, underwent, and suffered all pleasant or unpleasant occurrences arising from divine powers, men, or animals."
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

documents.text: Of his equanimity through those years of austerity: "his heart was pure like the water...in autumn; nothing could soil him like the leaf of a lotus; his senses were well protected like those of a tortoise; he was single and alone like the horn of a rhinoceros; he was free like a bird...strong like a bull, difficult to attack like a lion, steady and firm like Mount Mandara, deep like the ocean, mild like the moon...pure like excellent gold...like a well-kindled fire he shone in his splendour."
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

documents.text: After thirteen years of ascetic practice, "outside of the town Grimbhikagrama on the bank of the river Rigupalika...under a Sal tree...in a squatting position with joined heels, exposing himself to the heat of the sun, after fasting two and a half days without drinking water, being engaged in deep meditation, (he) reached the highest knowledge and intuition, called Kevala, which is infinite, supreme, unobstructed, unimpeded, complete, and full." As a Kevalin, the text says he then "knew and saw all conditions of the world."
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

documents.text: Mahavira taught for thirty more years before dying at Pava. The text records his community at the time of his death as "an excellent community of fourteen thousand Sramanas...thirty-six thousand nuns...one hundred and fifty-nine thousand lay votaries...three hundred and eighteen thousand female lay votaries," among other named ranks of advanced disciples — the fourfold Jain community (monks, nuns, laymen, laywomen) that continues today.
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

documents.text: Mahavira (Vardhamana), the 24th and last Tirthakara of this era in Jain tradition, is the subject of the Kalpa Sutra's Life of Mahavira (Lectures 1-5). This entry draws only on Lecture 2 (conception) and Lecture 5 (naming, renunciation, austerities, enlightenment, death, community) of Hermann Jacobi's 1884 translation, Sacred Books of the East vol. 22, public domain. The Kalpa Sutra's earlier lectures and its separate Life of Parsva are not reproduced here.
documents.source_name: Jaina Sutras, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 22), translated by Hermann Jacobi (1884), Kalpa Sutra: Life of Mahavira

documents.text: Shivaji was born in the hill-fort of Shivneri, near Junnar in Pune district. He was named after a local deity, the goddess Shivai.
documents.source_name: Shivaji The Great

documents.text: Shivaji's mother Jijabai deeply influenced his life and upbringing, teaching him the stories of the Ramayana and Mahabharata.
documents.source_name: Shivaji The Great

documents.text: He founded the Maratha Empire. His vision was to establish Hindavi Swarajya, a self-rule of Hindu people.
documents.source_name: Shivaji The Great
